div.formula: c ks = u k γ ks+ -sv * k γ ks-, (C36) c -k-s = u k γ † ks-+ sv * k γ † ks+ . (C37)
div.p: It is important to note that the Bogoliubov quasiparticle operators are not independent and satisfy a conjugate relation
div.formula: γ † ksη = γ -k-s-η . (C38)
div.p: Each spinful Bogoliubov quasiparticle always has its conjugate alternative with opposite spin and energy. Therefore, we can have 4 N combinations of complete and orthogonal quasiparticle bases, where N describes the size of the normal metal or the number of kinetic momenta. In principle, we can choose any OBSs, such as spin-momentum OBSs (γ k↑+ , γ k↓+ ) and (γ k↑-, γ k↓-), or Nambu-momentum OBSs (γ k↑+ , γ k↑-) and (γ k↓+ , γ k↓-) for k in the entire Brillouin zone. Furthermore, by choosing the OBS (γ k↑+ , γ k↓-, γ k↓+ , γ k↓-) for k in the half Brillouin zone, the semiconductor analogy of the superconductor produces dramatic features in various spectroscopies of the superconducting condensate [11].

a. Spin-momentum OBSs STEP I): Let us first study the two spin-momentum OBSs conjugate with each other (γ k↑+ , γ k↓+ ) and (γ k↑-, γ k↓-) for k in the whole Brillouin zone, whose elements satisfy the anticommutation relation {γ ksη , γ k ′ s ′ η } = δ k,k ′ δ s,s ′ . We can derive the corresponding Hamiltonian from the BdG Hamiltonian (C33) by transforming the other quasiparticles into the chosen OBSs using the conjugate relation (C38)
div.formula: H L = ks E ks+ γ † ks+ γ ks+ + E L + = ks E ks-γ † ks-γ ks-+ E L -.(C39)
div.p: The energy constants E L + and E L -arise from the mathematical transformation of the BdG Hamiltonian (C33) into the Hamiltonian (C39) using the OBSs (γ k↑+ , γ k↓+ ) and (γ k↑-, γ k↓-) Solving these equations, we obtain the corresponding EVSs + + E k1s1+ + E k2s2+ , and so on, up to E L + + ks E ks+ , respectively, where k i , s i are required to satisfy the Pauli exclusion principle for many Bogoliubov quasiparticles. The ground state is the state with the lowest energy among these 4 N states. In this case, the ground state is the Fermi sea, which corresponds to filling all the negative-energy Bogoliubov quasiparticles in the OBS (γ k↑+ , γ k↓+ ) starting from the EVS |V ⟩ + . Similarly, for the OBS (γ k↑-, γ k↓-), the ground state is given by filling all the negative-energy Bogoliubov quasiparticles in the OBS (γ k↑-, γ k↓-) starting from the EVS |V ⟩ -. Therefore, the superconducting ground state can be written as
div.formula: E L + = E + 1 2 ks E ks-,(C40)
E L -= E + 1 2 ks E ks+ .(
div.p: with energy
div.formula: E G = E L + + E ks+ <0 E ks+ = E L -+ E ks-<0 E ks-. (C47)
div.p: When h L < ∆, we have E ks+ > 0 and E ks-< 0. In this case, the ground state can be written as the EVS |V ⟩ + with energy
div.formula: E G = E L + = E + 1 2
div.p: ks E ks-. PROPERTY I): The relation between the EVSs |V ⟩ + and |V ⟩ -can be derived as follows.
For h L = 0, the ground state can be expressed in terms of two spin-momentum OBSs as
Again, the EVSs |V ⟩ + and |V ⟩ - form a conjugate loop
div.formula: |V ⟩ η = k γ † k↑-η γ † -k↓-η |V ⟩ -η ,(C48)
div.p: where η = -of Eq. (C48) is derived from the η = + of Eq. (C48) via the conjugate relation (C38).

b. Nambu-momentum OBSs STEP I): Then, we study the Nambu-momentum OBSs conjugate with each other, denoted as (γ k↑+ , γ k↑-) and (γ k↓+ , γ k↓-) for k in the entire Brillouin zone, whose elements anticommutate with each other, i.e., {γ ksη , γ k ′ sη ′ } = δ k,k ′ δ η,η ′ . The corresponding Hamiltonian can be derived from the BdG Hamiltonian (C33) by transforming the remaining quasiparticles into the chosen Nambu-momentum OBSs using the conjugate relation (C38)
div.formula: H L = kη E k↑η γ † k↑η γ k↑η + E L ↑ = kη E k↓η γ † k↓η γ k↓η + E L ↓ ,(C49)
div.p: where E L ↑ and E L ↓ are energy constants resulting from the mathematical transformation of the BdG Hamiltonian (C33) into the form (C49) using the Nambu-momentum operators (γ k↑+ , γ k↑-) and (γ k↓+ , γ k↓-) We can then obtain the corresponding EVSs [9,10] |V
div.formula: E L ↑ = E + 1 2 kη E k↓η ,(C50)
E L ↓ = E + 1 2 kη E k↑η .(
div.p: The EVS |V ⟩ ↑ (|V ⟩ ↓ ) contains all spin down (up) electrons. STEP III): Let us express the superconducting ground state using the Nambu-momentum OBSs -(γ k↑+ , γ k↑-) for all k. In a superconducting system, there are
div.formula: 4 N eigenstates: C(2N, 0) EVS |V ⟩ ↑ , C(2N, 1) one- Bogoliubon states γ † k1↑η1 |V ⟩ ↑ , C(2N, 2) two-Bogoliubon states γ † k1↑η1 γ † k2↑η2 |V ⟩ ↑ ,
L ↑ , E L ↑ + E k1↑η1 , E L ↑ + E k1↑η1 + E k2↑η2
div.p: , and so on, up to E L ↑ + kη E k↑η , respectively. Here, k i , η i are required to satisfy the Pauli exclusion principle for many Bogoliubov quasiparticles. The ground state is the state with the lowest energy among these 4 N eigenstates. It corresponds to filling all negative-energy Bogoliubov quasiparticles of the Nambu-momentum OBS (γ k↑+ , γ k↑-) into the EVS |V ⟩ ↑ . The same applies to the Nambu-momentum OBS (γ k↓+ , γ k↓-) for all k, using the EVS (C55). Thus, the superconducting ground state is given by
with energy
div.formula: E G = E L ↑ + E k↑η <0 E k↑η = E L ↓ + E k↓η <0 E k↓η . (C57)
div.p: When h L < ∆, we have E ks+ > 0 and E ks-< 0. By substituting Eqs. (C54) and (C55), the ground state (C56) can be written as
, which is equivalent to Eq. (C46) after substituting the Bogoliubov quasiparticle operator (??).
PROPERTY I): To establish the relation between EVSs (C54) and (C55), we consider the case when h L = 0. In this scenario, the ground state can be expressed in terms of the Nambu-momentum OBSs |G⟩ =
Using the conjugate relation (C38), we find that the eigenstates |V ⟩ ↑ and |V ⟩ ↓ also form a conjugate loop:
div.formula: |V ⟩ s = k γ † k-s+ γ † k-s-|V ⟩ -s . (C58)
div.p: Here, the EVS |V ⟩ s contains all the Bogoliubov quasiparticles with energies E k-sη .

c. Semiconductor analogy STEP I): Next, the semiconductor analogy is revisited, considering a complete basis consisting of two spins and two Nambu operators for the half Brillouin zone. Hereafter, we study two semiconductor-analogy OBSs conjugate with each other, i.e., k x > 0 OBS (γ k↑+ , γ k↑-, γ k↓+ , γ k↓-) for k in the k x > 0 half Brillouin zone and k x < 0 OBS (γ k↑+ , γ k↑-, γ k↓+ , γ k↓-) for k in the k x < 0 half Brillouin zone. These quasiparticle operators satisfy the anticommutation relation {γ ksη , γ k ′ s ′ η ′ } = δ k,k ′ δ η,η ′ δ s,s ′ when k is forced to be half Brillouin zone. The corresponding Hamiltonian can be derived from the BdG Hamiltonian (C33) by transforming the remaining quasiparticles into the chosen OBSs using the conjugate relation (C38). The Hamiltonian is given by
div.formula: H L = kx>0 sη E k↑η γ † k↑η γ k↑η + E L > (C59) = kx<0 sη E ksη γ † ksη γ k↓η + E L < ,
div.p: where the energy constants E L > and E L < arise from the mathematical transformation of the BdG Hamiltonian (C33) into the Hamiltonian (C59) using the chosen OBSs. The specific expressions for E L > and E L < are given as
div.formula: E L > = E + 1 2 kx<0 sη E k↓η ,(C60)
E L < = E + 1 2 kx>0 sη E k↑η .(C61)
div.p: STEP II): The EVSs of the two semiconductor-analogy OBSs, i.e., k x > 0 OBS (γ k↑+ , γ k↑-, γ k↓+ , γ k↓-) for k in the k x > 0 half Brillouin zone and k x > 0 OBS(γ k↑+ , γ k↑-, γ k↓+ , γ k↓-) for k in the k x < 0 half Brillouin zone, are, respectively, defined by
div.formula: γ ksη |V ⟩ > = 0, for k x > 0 and all s, η,(C62)
div.p: γ ksη |V ⟩ < = 0 for k x < 0 and all s, η.
Then, we obtain the corresponding EVSs

The EVS |V ⟩ > (|V ⟩ < ) contains all k x < 0 (k x > 0) electrons. STEP III): Let us first work on the semiconductoranalogy OBS (γ k↑+ , γ k↑-, γ k↓+ , γ k↓-) for k in the k x > 0 half Brillouin zone.
The system has a total of 4 N eigenstates.
These eigenstates in-

=   kx>0 E ksη <0 γ † ksη   |V ⟩ > =   kx<0 E ksη <0 γ † ksη   |V ⟩ < , (C66)
div.p: with energy

div.p: When h L < ∆, we have E ks+ > 0 and E ks-< 0.
By substituting the EVSs (C64) and (C65), the ground state (C66) can be represented as

Appendix D: Derivation of effective vacuum states
In this section, we derive the EVSs (or Fermi sea and sky), the Eq. ( 10) in the main text.
For briefness, we combine l (n) and s as a (b). Then, the quasiparticles [Eq. (2)] can be rewritten into

which is the (n + 1)th term of the left-hand side of Eq. (D9). Therefore, the superconducting cloud matrix (D13) is our solution for EVSs.

Then, Eq. (D13) becomes
Notably, the cloud matrix (D37) is off-diagonal in spin space. Thus, the Cooper-like pair consists of two superconducting clouds with different spins. By substitution of Eq. (D37), Eq. (D15) becomes
with
Here, we specialize the spin subscript of field operators c † ks for better description of the Cooper-like pair. Then, we obtain

div.p: Alternatively, the Fermi sea and sky can be rewritten into a compact form
Though Cη can be non-Hermitian, we can still diagonalize it by singular value decomposition
div.formula: Cη = U ↑η A η U + ↓η ,(D43)
div.p: where U sη is unitary matrix and A η is diagonal matrix with non-negative real Bogoliubov coefficients A η nn . Thus, EVS (D42) has Cooper-like pair form
div.formula: |V ⟩ η = 1 N 1/2 η N k=1 1 + a † k↑η A η kk a † k↓η |0⟩,(D44)
div.p: with
The complete and orthogonal superconducting spin cloud operators are given by
div.formula: a † k↑η = n c † n↑ (U ↑η ) nk ,(D46)
a † k↓η = n (U + ↓η ) kn c † n↓ .(D47)
div.p: The superconducting spin cloud operators (D46) and (D47) show how the electrons in the nonuniform superconductor pair with each other. The superconducting spin cloud picture of the ground state describes the quantum entanglement of the hybrid system. This is a widely entangled system as shown by the superconducting cloud matrix (D40), which might be interesting for future quantum computation.

div.head: Appendix E: Microscopic derivation of the tunneling current
div.p: In this subsection, we derive the tunneling current between tip and quantum-dot Josephson junction from a microscopic perspective, i.e., Eq. ( 21) in the main text.
The tip electrons are described by the non-interacting Hamiltonian
div.formula: H t = ps E ps c † ps c ps ,(E1)
div.p: where c ps is the field operator of electron in the tip with momentum p, spin s, and energy E p . The electron tunneling Hamiltonian between the quantum dot and the tip can be expressed as follows For tip electrons, we have κ i = α i = (p i , s i ), corresponding to fκi = f αi = c p i ,si . For the quantum-dot Josephson junction, we have κ i = λ i = (l i , s i , η i ), corresponding to fκi = f λi = γ li,si,ηi . As shown in Eq. (E12), we are only interested in the time evolution of the diagonal and tip components of the one-body density matrix, i.e., ϱ αα (t) = ϱ α (t), given by By means of Eq. (E15), the tunneling current is given by In the presence of the applied voltage, V , we assume . Finally, we obtain the tunneling current as follows
div.formula: V
I T = e α J α (ρ),(E30) with
ϱ ps = f (E p -V ), ϱ lsη = f (E lsη ),(
I T = 2πe h plsη f (E lsη )[1 -f (E p -V )]|T ps lsη | 2 δ(E p -E lsη ) - 2πe h plsη f (E p -V )[1 -f (E lsη )]|T ps lsη | 2 δ(E p -E lsη ).
div.p: (E38) This is the tunneling current between tip and quantumdot Josephson junction from a microscopic perspective, given by Eq. ( 21) in the main text.

figure.head: Figure 1 .
figure.figDesc: Figure 1. Conjugate loop and parity change of effective vacuum states (EVSs). Here, we exclude spin-flip effect and assume the case of E ls+ > 0 and E ls-< 0, where Fermi sea and sky become the EVSs |V ⟩+ and |V ⟩-, respectively. (a) Fermi sea and sky -the EVSs of η = + and η = -orthonormal basis sets [Eq. (10)], respectively, contain all η = -and η = + Bogoliubons [Eq. (14)] and therefore form a conjugate loop. (b) Parity change of the EVSs due to the exchange of a pair of basis states conjugate with each other, e.g., Andreev operators γ 1↓+ and γ 1↑-.

figure.head: Figure 2 .
figure.figDesc: Figure 2. The quasiparticle occupancy controversy in quantum dot. Though there are four Andreev levels in Nambu space, it only poses four states given in the reduced conjugate loops rather than 2 4 . For the rest 12 occupancies, five are identical to the four (same color boxes), while seven do not exist (red-dash boxes).
(b)]. For Γ = 0 (blue box and curve), we have γ s+ = d s and γ s-= d † -s (see Appendix C 1), i.e., |D 1s+ | 2 = 1

figure.head: Figure 3 .
figure.figDesc: Figure 3. (a) Sketch of a quantum dot (QD) formed in a nanowire and tunnel coupled to two superconductors (SCs). (b) The zero magnetic field differential conductance, dI/dV , as a function of the voltage difference, V . In the absence of dot-lead tunnel coupling, the tip electron can not tunnel couple to γs-subgap quasiparticle (blue box), and hence there is not resonance at V = -ϵD (blue curve). While we find a pair of asymmetric resonances corresponding to two Andreev levels for Γ ̸ = 0 (red box and curve). The positive peak is higher than the negative one due to |D1s+| 2 > |D1s-| 2 , responsible for the particle-hole asymmetry in the subgap quasiparticles. (b-e) The finite magnetic field differential conductance, dI/dV , as a function of the voltage difference, V and (c) Γ, (d) ϵD, (e) ϕ, and (f) hD, where other parameters are given by Γ = 0.2, ϵD = 0.1, ϕ = π/2, hD = 0.3, and hL = 0.

figure.head: Figure 4 .
figure.figDesc: Figure 4. The QPT of the spin-split quantum dot in different OBSs (a) (γ ↑+ , γ ↓+ ), (b) (γ ↑-, γ ↓-), (c) (γ ↑+ , γ ↑-), and (d) (γ ↓+ , γ ↓-). The ground state (GS) is equal to adding all negative basis states to the corresponding EVS and undergoes a parity changes at ϵD = hD, where both ϵD and hD is asssumed to be positive. (e,f) The EVSs of two OBSs conjugate with each other, for example (e) |V ⟩± [Eq. (C8)] and (f) |V ⟩ ↑/↓ [Eq. (C14)], form a conjugate loop.

figure.head: Figure 5 .
figure.figDesc: Figure 5. Illustration of Eq. (C25). Panels (a) and (b) correspond to the construction of the ground state from Fermi sea and sky, respectively.

C25) When we work in the spin-momentum OBS (γ k↑+ , γ k↓+ ), this product adds spin-down electrons with 0 < ϵ k < h L (γ † k↓+ = c † k↓ ) and removes spin-up electrons with energy in the interval -h L < ϵ -k < 0 (γ † k↓+ = c -k↑ ) [Fig. 5 (a)]. When we work in another spin-momentum OBS (γ k↑-, γ k↓-), this product removes spin-down electrons with energy in the interval ϵ k > h L (γ † k↑-= c -k↓ ), adds spin-up electrons with 0 < ϵ k < -h L (γ † k↑-= c † k↑ ), removes spin-up electrons with energy in the interval ϵ k > 0 (γ † k↓-= c -k↑ ), and adds spin-down electrons with ϵ k < 0 (γ † k↓-= c † k↓ ) [Fig. 5 (b)]. These two combinations of the complete and orthogonal basis states obtain the same ground state as shown in Fig. 5. following our new logic. Let us calculate the superconducting ground state in term of the η = + OBSs (γ l⇑+ , γ l⇓+ ) for all l. The superconductor has 4 N eigenstates. These states exhibit entanglement between electrons with energies E k↑ and E -k↓ , which form Cooper pairs. STEP III): Let us calculate the superconducting ground state in term of the spin-momentum OBS (γ k↑+ , γ k↓+ ).The superconductor has 4 N eigenstates. These eigenstates include C(2N, 0)EVS |V ⟩ + , C(2N, 1) one-Bogoliubon states γ † k1s1+ |V ⟩ + , C(2N, 2) two-Bogoliubon states γ † k1s1+ γ † k2s2+ |V ⟩ + ,and so on, up to C(2N, 2N ) 2N -Bogoliubon states ( ks γ † ks+ )|V ⟩ + . The corresponding energies of these states are E L + , E L + + E k1s1+ , E L
and so on, up to C(2N, 2N ) 2N -Bogoliubon state ( kη γ † k↑η )|V ⟩ ↑ . The corresponding energies are E

+ kx>0 sη E ksη , respectively, where k i , s i , η i are required to satisfy the Pauli exclusion principle for many Bogoliubov quasiparticles. The ground state corresponds to the lowest energy state among these 4 N states, and it represents a filled sea of negative Bogoliubov quasiparticles in this OBS. Similarly, for the OBS (γ k↑+ , γ k↑-, γ k↓+ , γ k↓-) for k in the k x < 0 half Brillouin zone, we have a similar set of eigenstates and energies. The superconducting ground state is given by |G⟩

biblStruct.note: denoted as P = (-1) ns c † ns c ns , provides information about the number of electrons in a system. It assigns an eigenvalue of -1 if the number of electrons is odd and +1 if the number is even. While the conservation of electron number is not applicable in a superconductor, the conservation of fermionic parity, denoted as [P, H] = 0, holds true. This conservation of fermionic parity implies that if the initial state of the system has an even (odd) number of fermions, the final state will also have an even (odd) number of fermions for a fixed set of system parameters. However, it is important to note that the ground state of a superconductor can undergo changes in fermionic parity with variations in system parameters, such as Coulomb interaction, magnetic field, and phase difference in Josephson junctions.
